name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Data Transmission Without Encryption
embedding_text: network data transmission plaintext weak encryption sensitive information in transit interception tampering
description: Information sent over a network can be compromised while in transit. An attacker may be able to read or modify the contents if the data are sent in plaintext or are weakly encrypted.
potential_mitigations: Use TLS/SSL to encrypt all network communications handling sensitive data
Ensure strong cipher suites with forward secrecy are configured for encryption
Avoid sending credentials or tokens in URLs or query parameters
Implement certificate pinning to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks

name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Insufficient Session-ID Length
embedding_text: j2ee application insufficient session id length brute force session hijacking session identifier brute force guessing weak session token
description: The J2EE application is configured to use an insufficient session ID length.
potential_mitigations: configure session identifiers to be at least 128 bits long to prevent brute force guessing attacks
implement a short inactive session timeout to reduce the number of valid session identifiers available for guessing
ensure session identifiers have sufficient entropy to make brute force attacks computationally infeasible
use cryptographically secure random session id generation methods like java secure random

name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
embedding_text: web application error page sensitive information disclosure default error page 4xx 5xx exception handler information leakage
description: The default error page of a web application should not display sensitive information about the product.
potential_mitigations: configure custom error pages for all HTTP status codes
implement last-resort exception handler to suppress stack traces
avoid processing errors or masking them to prevent information exposure
ensure error responses do not reveal system details or internal paths

name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Entity Bean Declared Remote
embedding_text: java bean remote interface exposes methods read modify sensitive data validation
description: When an application exposes a remote interface for an entity bean, it might also expose methods that get or set the bean's data. These methods could be leveraged to read sensitive information, or to change data in ways that violate the application's expectations, potentially leading to other vulnerabilities.
potential_mitigations: avoid exposing bean methods remotely unless absolutely necessary
ensure sensitive fields are not exposed in remote interfaces
implement strict input validation for all data modification operations
restrict access to bean methods using proper access control

name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Weak Access Permissions for EJB Methods
embedding_text: enterprise java beans method permissions any role elevated access rights attacker exploit permissions ejb deployment descriptor method permissions grant access anyone role access control
description: If elevated access rights are assigned to EJB methods, then an attacker can take advantage of the permissions to exploit the product.
potential_mitigations: assign enterprise java beans method permissions using principle of least privilege
avoid granting method access to special ANYONE role in deployment descriptor
ensure access control restrictions are applied to ejb methods
review and restrict method permissions in ejb deployment configuration

name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Creating Debug Binary
embedding_text: debug binaries production verbose error messages system details attackers exposure asp net
description: Debugging messages help attackers learn about the system and plan a form of attack.
potential_mitigations: disable debug mode in production by setting debug=false in asp net configuration files
remove debug binaries from production environments before deployment
restrict detailed error messages to development environments only
use custom error pages in production to avoid exposing system internals

name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
embedding_text: asp.net application custom error pages prevent information leakage framework default responses mining attackers
description: An ASP .NET application must enable custom error pages in order to prevent attackers from mining information from the framework's built-in responses.
potential_mitigations: configure asp.net to use custom error pages instead of default framework responses
avoid masking or processing errors in ways that reveal sensitive system details
validate all return values to prevent exposure of internal system information
handle exceptions in source code rather than relying on default framework behavior

name: ASP.NET Misconfiguration: Password in Configuration File
embedding_text: storing plaintext passwords in configuration files exposed credentials unauthorized access
description: Storing a plaintext password in a configuration file allows anyone who can read the file access to the password-protected resource making them an easy target for attackers.
potential_mitigations: encrypt passwords before storing them in configuration files
use standard encryption APIs for credential storage
apply industry-accepted algorithms like AES for password encryption
restrict file permissions to prevent unauthorized reads of configuration files

name: Compiler Removal of Code to Clear Buffers
embedding_text: compiler optimization dead store removal memory scrubbing sensitive data clearing overwrite optimization leaves secret data in memory
description: Sensitive memory is cleared according to the source code, but compiler optimizations leave the memory untouched when it is not read from again, aka dead store removal.
potential_mitigations: store sensitive data in volatile memory locations when possible
configure compiler to disable dead store removal for memory scrubbing operations
use compiler flags like -fno-dead-code-elimination to preserve scrubbing writes
encrypt sensitive data at rest to mitigate exposure from compiler optimizations

name: External Control of System or Configuration Setting
embedding_text: user input controls system settings configuration externally controlled settings malicious configuration changes privilege escalation
description: One or more system settings or configuration elements can be externally controlled by a user.
potential_mitigations: sanitize and validate all user-provided configuration inputs using allowlists
store and manage configuration settings server-side with strict access controls
implement role-based access control to restrict who can modify system settings
use environment variables or secure configuration files with proper permissions

name: Improper Input Validation
embedding_text: input validation missing unvalidated data user input metadata raw data string number parameter file contents header size length frequency price rate operations time offset position data structure hash table associative array type consistency domain rules business logic authenticity source cryptographic signature
description: The product receives input or data, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input has the properties that are required to process the data safely and correctly.
potential_mitigations: validate all inputs including parameters cookies network data environment variables and file contents using an accept known good strategy
use a formal parser or language theoretic security approach to enforce input boundaries between raw data and internal representations
apply server side validation for all inputs even if client side checks exist to prevent bypass via modified requests
canonicalize and decode inputs before validation to prevent double encoding attacks and ensure consistent validation

name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that is intended to identify a file or directory that is located underneath a restricted parent directory, but the product does not properly neutralize special elements within the pathname that can cause the pathname to resolve to a location that is outside of the restricted directory.
potential_mitigations: use strict allowlist validation for file paths and reject any paths containing path traversal sequences like ../ or /
canonicalize and validate user input paths using built-in functions like realpath or getCanonicalPath before processing
